words: Yeah yeah. The job you know they finished one thing and then they might do another thing. So it lasted until the worst of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was over there. Was there much opportunity for get togethers and park during that time, or was that real different than it had been over? These other place?

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Well, during the depression, no. Nobody could afford to get together. And they didn't, everybody was concerned with getting enough wood feed and enough food and keeping things together more or less, you know, during the depression. But now, after the depression broke, why then everybody we all went to the dead all the years that we were there, except, you know, during the depression, we went to the schoolhouse either Friday or Saturday night and played cards.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: And instead of everyone taking something like we used to, you know, where I went to school, why they appointed two ladies. One would bring cake and, and bring sandwiches. And that was enough for the group.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that was enough for the group.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: My little Clem learned to play pinochle when he was in the first grade, and some of them used to kick about playing with him. You know, he got right up there and played at the table, right with the other partners, and some of them, you know, would sit down and he'd sit down across them and got to play a few.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Anyhow, after he played a little while, they found out he wasn't so bad to play with, and he was lucky. I never saw anything like it. He might bid crazy, but. And the other fella, you know, and wonder where his brains were. But anyhow. And Clem said, you know what he wanted by, the other fella would have it and he'd lay down.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Or his. Oh, dear, he'd have, 100 cases there, a thousand aces and, pinochle and. Oh, you never saw such a mess. I tell you, he was spoiled rotten for a while because he was so lucky. But cute little rascal. And of course, the other children are all nuts. They played right along with the rest of it.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Clem was three years younger than Ethel and. And if Alan and Don and Jerry and Anna all played. So anyway. Yeah, they played pinochle for years and years and years. We must have got off of they did that much practice everything and.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were in park, were there other women that had small children at the same time?

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Yeah. When my first little girl had a twin, Mrs. Austin's daughter had a little girl the next day and didn't know hers was the day before. And Anna's and it was the 15th of February, American girl on the 14th of February. And those other girls had lots of fun together. They grew up together. I guess they haven't seen each other now for a long time.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: But anyhow, Marjorie lived in Spokane for quite a long time, and whenever she'd come down, you know, we come and visit Diana. But I don't know where she is now. I haven't heard of her. Anna may know where she is. I don't know.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: But anyhow, they, When Fleming started the school, there were four other little children there. Start to school in the community, and then one family moved away. And that was huge. And, and by Christmas time, another family moved away. We kind of hated to lose that family. They had the most kids in school, and they moved to dairy.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: And now that little girl works up here in the the people that moved away there. A little girl works up here in the antelope in, in Cambridge. Oh. Where they had booths over the counter. So that's the way she turned out.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: And then Engels had a little one. They had one after that. After I finished teaching and park, their little girl started to school.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: She was the one that told you drove the truck and all the water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the community was largely Norwegian wasn't it. Park.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Yeah. Yeah. I just and you didn't want to dare say this is a Swedish community there. They felt that very strongly that this was a Norwegian community.

words: Did you go to the church there.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: No I never, never went to the church. But once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a Lutheran church was.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Yeah. Yeah. Right. It was built by no nation. It was for many years old when we moved over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make you feel apart from most of the community and that you were different. Background or did that matter at all?

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: It didn't seem to matter. Not at all. You know. No, there was, a nurse in the community who.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: We were the only two educated workers that. Yeah.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: And they moved out quite a long time before I left the community.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she. Did she practice? While she was There, would she would help?

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Yeah, if you needed. I know if I had pneumonia. And we took her up there to their home for a little while. A couple of weeks.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: You know, she had her own, family. She couldn't leave home. But anyhow, she would if you were really sick or she take you in and take care of you. Shoot. Just a real nice person.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first have dealings with, Frank Brocke and. And, the bank. Oh, well.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: All the time it seemed like. I know, And the first time ever, I went to the bank, and Ben had been there quite a few times before. Well, I met, Ole Bohman, and that was the man that had taken Frank into the bank with him. And my. He seemed like such a nice one. And, you know, when I got out someplace else and said, what a nice fellow Mr. Bohman seemed to be, they said, well, yeah, he's a nice guy.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: As long as you pay your debts. But I'll tell you right now he can be something else. And, you know, I've often thought how true that was. But anyhow, he was always nice to us. Well, Mr. Brocke, he's always been wonderful to you. But, Mr. Brocke he was, years and years young. Really, Mr. Bohman? Mr. Bohman had the bank first.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: He was the sole businessman. It was in. I don't know whether it was any place else or not, but it was in Deary. First one. I knew about it. And then we moved to Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did, did Ole Bohman. Was he always willing to stand by your husband and help? And help?

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Well, we never did ask for very much use. Ask for $50 or $60 or something. You know? But, he was always wonderful about it. Always. Both of them were always wonderful. And more times than times when we could, when he came, do we couldn't pay it. But anyhow, they'd wait a little while on us. But then they always had, even for that much.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: They always knew how many cows and how many horses, and how many hogs you had and what equipment you had, you know. And if if you had intended to beat the whole thing, you know, where they were. They sold you out. But anyhow, like Frank says on his that tape that they never unless somebody did act awful or they never did try to sell them out.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: And I think that's very true, because that's just the way it seemed to me like that. If you anywhere near tried to treat them decently, you know, where they would help you and not push you too hard either. But anyhow, both of them kind of had a way, you know, talking to you that, well, if you sell a couple of those cows, why you bring us the money and we go on, you know, for a little while longer and something like that.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: And I thought it was wonderful. I always did, because here at kindred, they never were like that, just never. I know one time after I married Vester that, the banker here was Kaneckaberg. For years, they had a mr. Carroll ever before, and he was a mighty nice fellow. I know my father dealt with him several times, but anyhow, they got this Mr. Kaneckaberg in there, and after Mr. and I were married, Mr..

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Had a bunch of, cows that was fattening to sell. And anyhow, by the time the note came down that he borrowed from the bank, the cattle weren't really top shape. You want to feed him a couple of weeks longer?

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: And now this has been about 20 years ago. And at that time when you when you sold cattle, you know, they were, Well, the buyers would think, well, they like to be fed a couple of weeks longer. We can't pay that much for them, you know, not quite prime or, they're real fit. You know, we get them pretty good price and they'd talk it over, you know, together.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: And. And sometimes you got almost nothing for something after they looked at it. Well, anyhow. Kaneckaberg wouldn't help Vester. He borrowed $200 to buy feed for them, to feed them. And so he went back and he said he'd like to buy some more food. He said they're not quite ready to go to the market. He says, I believe in two weeks that they be prime and going to burn one one monitoring.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: So he sold them. He never dealt with a withdrawal. I tried to get him to. He finally did go to it though. But anyhow, he sold those cattle and he got a terrible low price for them. I tell you, the things he thought of Mr. can make a burden weren't nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he dealt with them before to that and.

words: Quite late right here in the community.

words: Superintendent of the, well, I guess clerk.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Of the board. Yeah, right. He was a stinker there, too, I'll tell you. He signed a couple of three of my contracts, and. Oh, boy, I sure, sure never was too well pleased with him. Anyhow, he got in the banking business. He his wife was there, and and and they come to the senior citizen club.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: But anyhow, I don't, visit with them or not. I don't,

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Well, let's say I just leave him alone. I don't try to befriend them or I don't try to do anything wrong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: John Lee. It's funny because, I've always had the idea since talking to Mr. Brocke and a few other people around, that the difference in having a bank that was, that was really concerned about the welfare of the people and their community.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Yes, sir. The banker makes all the difference in the world for the community, and it's just one of those things. But Bert was just like that. That's all he anyhow. You know, another year or two, I got Mr. Convinced. He was afraid, you know, that if if he went out of his community and when you see Troy was clear out of our dealings here, you got nothing in Troy.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: You go to Lawson or go to Kendrick or go to our Athena. Well, we I finally got him to go up to Mr. Brock and talk to him. And they got along just wonderfully from the start. Just. Oh, well, Mr. couldn't even believe it himself. He was so happy with him. They were just happy with each other because Frank always acted like he just thought Mr. was wonderful, and Dexter sure thought that of him.

words: Do you know any of other other families? Would you say that that there are many families that you know of that do? Students.

words: They're a model. Students for a.

words: In the Troy Bank. And if they got a job offered, you know, let's say Richland or Moses Lake or back East Bank, Troy, you'd it was impossible to believe that the as long as Frank was there, your family, oddballs he had depositing their money there and using it from the Troy bank. And you'd ask those kids in Pullman, what are you buying it for, Troy for?

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: You don't have to pay for the checks. See, most banks charge $0.10 a check to get him cashed. And Frank, never charge that. Oh, it was just out of this world. When I went to Pullman, all the kids there were driving. Troy was sure, something different.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: Why, I said, that makes him, the chores. More than an hour later, I said, that's all right, then we're pay enough more. So anyhow, they paid me a good wage and I went to Poland from the girls house in Moscow, and I stayed in Pullman ten years. So I cooked for 11 years up there, and I cooked in all fraternity deck.

speaker: Eva Daniels
words: I got there from. But I just loved that work too.

words: Those kids whose mothers and fathers would come to visit, you know. And where did they come first? To the kitchen.

words: One time, one woman from Starbuck brought us two cases of asparagus raw. It was right from the bed. And so I cooked one mess, and I knew they wouldn't want a second machine or on top of that. words: And so they had another mess. In a few weeks. Boy, they wondered where that came from. Oh goodness, if they didn't have a lot of,
